matter.title: Grace C. Roe v. Summit Outfitters Co.
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe, a longtime employee of Summit Outfitters Co., alleges the company and supervisor John D. Kingsley subjected Grace to termination because of disability and in retaliation for reporting discrimination, in violation of the Maine Human Rights Act and Title VII.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe, a longtime employee of Summit Outfitters Co., alleges the company and supervisor John D. Kingsley subjected Grace to termination because of disability and in retaliation for reporting discrimination, in violation of the Maine Human Rights Act and Title VII.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe was hired by Summit Outfitters Co. on 2016-09-01 and, by all accounts, performed the job with reviews that were mixed. Grace is a member of a protected class on the basis of disability.

Grace alleges that, after raising concerns about discriminatory treatment, Roe was subjected to termination on 2024-09-14. The decision was carried out by Grace's supervisor, John D. Kingsley, and approved within the company. Grace contends the stated reasons were a pretext and that Grace was replaced by or treated worse than someone outside the protected class.

Grace timely filed a charge of discrimination with the Maine Human Rights Commission, which was cross-filed with the EEOC, and received a right-to-sue letter. Grace then filed this action in Sagadahoc County Superior Court seeking back pay, front pay, compensatory damages for emotional distress, and attorney fees. The matter is in active discovery.
undisputed_facts: Grace C. Roe was employed by Summit Outfitters Co. beginning on 2016-09-01.
Grace's employment ended, or the adverse action occurred, on or about 2024-09-14.
Grace filed a charge with the Maine Human Rights Commission and received a right-to-sue letter before filing suit.
disputed_facts: Whether the adverse action was motivated by disability or by a legitimate, non-discriminatory reason.
Whether the employer's stated reason for the termination is a pretext for discrimination or retaliation.
Whether Grace's internal complaint was protected activity that caused the adverse action.
The amount of back pay, front pay, and emotional-distress damages, and whether Grace mitigated by seeking comparable work.

timeline.event: Grace was hired by Summit Outfitters Co..
Complaint filed in Bath Superior Court after the right-to-sue letter issued.

third_parties.name: Maine Human Rights Commission
third_parties.relevance: Investigated the charge of discrimination and issued the right-to-sue letter after exhaustion under 5 M.R.S. § 4622.

third_parties.name: Desmond D. Poe
third_parties.relevance: Received the internal complaint and participated in the decision to take the adverse action.

third_parties.name: Omar C. Roe
third_parties.relevance: Observed the conduct and the differential treatment of the plaintiff.

exchanges.question: Tell me how your employment ended.
exchanges.answer: I worked at Summit Outfitters Co. for years with good reviews. After I spoke up about how I was being treated because of my disability, things changed and I was hit with termination. My supervisor, John, was the one who pushed it through.

exchanges.question: Did you report the discrimination before the adverse action?
exchanges.answer: Yes. I raised it internally with HR. Not long after, the termination came down. It felt like payback for complaining.

exchanges.question: What reason did the company give, and do you believe it?
exchanges.answer: They pointed to performance, but my reviews were mixed. I think the real reason was my disability, and I was replaced by or treated worse than someone outside my group.

exchanges.question: Did you go to the Maine Human Rights Commission?
exchanges.answer: Yes. I filed a charge with the Maine Human Rights Commission, it was cross-filed with the EEOC, and I got my right-to-sue letter. That is why we can be in court now.

exchanges.question: How has this affected you financially and personally?
exchanges.answer: I lost my salary of about $103027 a year plus benefits, and I have been looking for comparable work. The stress has been significant and I have been seeing a provider about it.

exchanges.question: What outcome are you looking for?
exchanges.answer: I want to be made whole: back pay, front pay, and the record corrected. I want the employer held accountable, but I will settle if the deal is fair.

goals: Obtain make-whole relief, including back pay and front pay, for the termination.
Establish that the employer and supervisor discriminated and retaliated in violation of the Maine Human Rights Act and Title VII.
Recover compensatory damages for emotional distress, plus attorney fees and costs.
priorities: Proving pretext and the causal link between the protected activity and the adverse action.
Correcting the employment record and protecting future job prospects.
Preserving leverage for a fair, timely settlement.
desired_outcomes: A judgment or settlement providing back pay, front pay, and compensatory damages.
A neutral or corrected reference and removal of adverse material from the personnel file.
non_starters: Any resolution that leaves the discrimination and retaliation unaddressed.

constraints.budget: Moderate; willing to litigate through summary judgment but open to mediation.
constraints.timeline: Wants the matter resolved without prolonged delay while back pay continues to accrue.

issues.issue: Whether the plaintiff exhausted administrative remedies and holds a valid right to sue
issues.governing_law: Maine Human Rights Act exhaustion, 5 M.R.S. § 4622
sub_questions: Was the charge timely filed with the Maine Human Rights Commission?
Did a right-to-sue letter issue before the complaint was filed?
issues.client_position: The charge was timely filed and a right-to-sue letter issued, so the claims are properly before the court.

issues.issue: Whether the plaintiff can establish a prima facie case of employment discrimination
issues.governing_law: Maine Human Rights Act, 5 M.R.S. § 4572; Title VII, 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2; McDonnell Douglas burden-shifting framework
sub_questions: Is the plaintiff a member of a protected class who was qualified for the position?
Did the plaintiff suffer an adverse employment action under circumstances giving rise to an inference of discrimination?
issues.client_position: The plaintiff was qualified, performing well, and suffered the adverse action because of disability.

issues.issue: The measure of damages and available civil penalties
issues.governing_law: Maine Human Rights Act remedies and civil penalties, 5 M.R.S. § 4613
sub_questions: What back pay, front pay, and compensatory damages are recoverable?
Are civil penal damages and attorney fees available, and did the plaintiff mitigate?
issues.client_position: The plaintiff is entitled to back pay, front pay, emotional-distress damages, a civil penalty, and attorney fees.

issues.issue: Whether the employer's stated reason is a pretext for discrimination
sub_questions: Has the employer articulated a legitimate, non-discriminatory reason?
Can the plaintiff show that reason is unworthy of credence or that discrimination was the real reason?
issues.client_position: The performance rationale is pretextual given reviews that were mixed and more favorable treatment of comparators.

issues.issue: Whether the adverse action was unlawful retaliation for protected activity
sub_questions: Did the plaintiff engage in protected activity by reporting discrimination?
Is there a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action?
issues.client_position: The adverse action followed closely after the internal complaint and was caused by it.

expert_opinions.topic: Present value of lost wages and benefits
expert_opinions.opinion: The present value of the plaintiff's lost back pay, front pay, and benefits is significant and reasonably quantifiable.
expert_opinions.basis: Earnings histories, benefit valuations, and discount-rate modeling.

expert_opinions.topic: Back pay, front pay, and loss of earning capacity
expert_opinions.opinion: The plaintiff's wage loss and diminished earning capacity are substantial given age, skills, and the local labor market.
expert_opinions.basis: Wage records, labor-market data, and a vocational assessment.

expert_opinions.topic: Performance evaluation and the basis for the employment decision
expert_opinions.opinion: The employer's evaluation process was facially neutral and consistently applied.
expert_opinions.basis: Personnel records, evaluation instruments, and comparator data.

evidence.title: Termination letter
evidence.description: The letter or notice memorializing the termination and the stated reason.

evidence.title: MHRC charge and right-to-sue letter
evidence.description: The Maine Human Rights Commission charge of discrimination and the issued right-to-sue letter.

evidence.title: Personnel file and performance reviews
evidence.description: The plaintiff's complete personnel file, including evaluations that were mixed.

evidence.title: Plaintiff's contemporaneous notes
evidence.description: The plaintiff's dated notes documenting the discriminatory conduct and the complaint.

evidence.title: Internal complaint emails
evidence.description: Emails reporting discrimination to HR and management before the adverse action.

evidence.title: Employee handbook and anti-discrimination policy
evidence.description: The handbook and EEO/anti-retaliation policies in effect during the employment.

amounts.label: Projected front pay
amounts.basis: Estimated future wage loss until reemployment or recovery.

amounts.label: Estimated attorney fees and statutory civil penalty
amounts.basis: Fee-shifting recovery and the civil penal damages under 5 M.R.S. § 4613.

amounts.label: Lost benefits value
amounts.basis: Value of lost health, retirement, and other benefits.

amounts.label: Lost back pay
amounts.basis: Wages lost from the adverse action to the present.

causes_of_action.claim: Employment discrimination under the Maine Human Rights Act
elements: Circumstances giving rise to an inference of discrimination
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Back pay, front pay, compensatory damages, a civil penalty, and attorney fees

causes_of_action.claim: Retaliation under the Maine Human Rights Act
elements: A causal connection between the two
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Back pay, front pay, compensatory damages, and attorney fees

elements: A timely charge and right-to-sue letter
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Back pay, front pay, compensatory damages, and attorney fees

elements: The supervisor aided or abetted that discrimination

affirmative_defenses.basis: The plaintiff did not exercise reasonable diligence to seek comparable substitute employment.
The employer would have made the same decision regardless of any protected activity or characteristic.
Evidence discovered after the action would have independently justified the termination, limiting back pay.

counterclaims.relief: Damages and injunctive relief for the disclosure of confidential company information.

discovery.description: Deposition of the plaintiff on the claims and damages.
Deposition of the supervisor who carried out the adverse action.
Subpoena to a subsequent employer on mitigation and post-termination earnings.
Requests for the personnel file, comparator records, and internal complaint emails.
First set of interrogatories on the decision-makers and the reasons for the adverse action.
Admissions on the timing of the complaint and the adverse action.

motions.title: Motion for partial summary judgment on retaliation
motions.relief: Judgment that the adverse action followed the protected activity.

motions.title: Motion to compel comparator data
motions.relief: An order compelling production of comparator and replacement records.

motions.title: Motion in limine to exclude me-too evidence
motions.relief: Exclusion of other employees' discrimination testimony.

motions.title: Motion for summary judgment
motions.relief: Judgment that the plaintiff cannot show pretext or causation.

docket.entry: Complaint filed following the Maine Human Rights Commission right-to-sue letter.
Scheduling order entered; discovery deadline set.
Order on the parties' discovery disputes regarding comparator data.
Summary judgment briefing completed and taken under advisement.
